content.text: How the Append Effect Works

The Append effect modifies the ARM request body before the resource is created or updated — it injects additional properties or array values into the request. The resource is created with the appended values rather than the original request values. Append runs synchronously at request time, unlike DeployIfNotExists which runs asynchronously after resource creation. The primary use cases are adding IP rules to network ACLs, enforcing specific tag values, and setting properties the requestor did not include in their deployment template.

attrs.alt: Append effect: property injection and conflict behavior diagram

content.text: Append vs Modify: Which to Use

Append and Modify are both mutation effects but behave differently. Append adds values to array fields or sets absent properties; it cannot remove existing values and conflicts with explicitly-set differing values produce a 403 error. Modify can add, replace, or remove properties and tags, and handles update operations as well as creates. For most use cases that were historically done with Append, Modify is the preferred modern effect — Append is mainly used for array field scenarios where you want to accumulate values (like adding required IP rules to a network ACL) rather than replace the entire field.
Capability
Append
Modify
Add a property value if absent
Yes
Yes
Replace an existing property value
No — conflict = 403
Yes — replaces with policy value
Add value to an existing array
Yes — merges
Yes (addOrReplace operation)
Remove a property
No
Yes (remove operation)
Works on existing resources (update)
Yes, if property is missing
Yes, full mutation on create and update
Requires managed identity
No
Yes — system- or user-assigned identity on the assignment
Effect on tags
Can append tag values
Add, replace, or remove tags
Append Policy Definition Structure

An Append effect definition uses the then.details array to specify one or more field-value pairs to inject. Each entry specifies a field path and the value to append. For array fields, the value must be an array — the policy engine merges the policy array into the request's existing array values.

The mode: Indexed setting means the policy only evaluates resource types that support tags and location — it skips resource group-level and subscription-level resources. Use mode: All when you need to evaluate all resource types including resource groups.
Conflict Behavior

When a request explicitly sets a field to value A and the Append policy wants to set the same field to value B, the result is a 403 conflict error. This is different from Modify, which silently overwrites. The conflict error is important to understand because it means Append is safe for additive enforcement but cannot be used to correct an explicitly-wrong value — that requires Modify or Deny.

# Scenario: Append policy sets tags['environment'] = 'prod'
# Developer creates storage account with tags['environment'] = 'staging'
# Result: 403 conflict

# Error message:
# (RequestDisallowedByPolicy) Resource was disallowed by policy.
# Policy identifiers: 'append-environment-tag'.
# Additional details: The request to 'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts' was denied
# because of conflicting field values in policy append operation.
# Field: tags['environment'], PolicyValue: prod, RequestValue: staging

# To avoid conflicts, scope the append condition to only fire when field is absent:
# policyRule.if: { field: "tags['environment']", exists: "false" }
# This way the append only fires when the tag is missing, not when it's set to wrong value.

# For correcting wrong values, use Modify instead of Append:
# then: { effect: modify, details: [{ operation: addOrReplace, field: ..., value: ... }] }

# Check if a policy is blocking a specific resource creation:
az policy state list \
  --resource /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/sa-myapp \
  --query '[].{Assignment:policyAssignmentName,State:complianceState,Action:policyDefinitionAction}' \
  -o table
Real-World Append Use Cases
Ensuring storage accounts include a corporate IP allowlist entry

Network ACL ipRules is an array — multiple entries are allowed. Append is the correct effect because you want to add the corporate IP range to whatever IP rules the deployer already specified, not replace them. If the developer specified [10.0.0.1/24] and the policy appends [10.1.0.0/16], the result is both entries in the array.

# Assign the append IP rule policy
az policy assignment create \
  --name append-corp-ip-rule \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --policy $DEF_ID \
  --params '{"requiredIpRange":{"value":"203.0.113.0/24"}}'

# Verify a newly created storage account received the appended IP rule
az storage account show \
  --name sa-myapp \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query 'networkRuleSet.ipRules' -o json
# Output: [{"action":"Allow","value":"10.0.0.1/24"},{"action":"Allow","value":"203.0.113.0/24"}]
Appending a required diagnosticSettings category

If an existing Append policy for diagnostic settings is in place and a Bicep template creates a storage account without diagnostic settings, the append will inject the required diagnostic category. However, if the template creates a diagnostic setting with some but not all required categories, the append will add the missing categories to the array.
Append vs Modify for Tags

Both Append and Modify can add tags. The difference is that Append can only add a tag when it is absent (using the exists: false condition) and will conflict if the tag exists with a different value. Modify with addOrReplace operation can add the tag if absent OR correct it if it has the wrong value. For most tag enforcement scenarios, Modify is the better choice — Append is appropriate only when you want to preserve whatever tag value the deployer sets and only fill in when no value was specified.
Terraform: Append Policy Assignment
Multiple Append Operations in One Policy

A single Append policy definition can specify multiple field-value pairs in the details array. Each entry is applied independently — if one field conflicts, the entire request fails with a 403. All entries in the details array are applied atomically; the policy engine does not partially apply some entries and skip others.
Append and Existing Resources

Append only fires on create and update operations. Resources that existed before the Append policy was assigned are not retroactively modified. If you need to apply the appended values to existing resources, you must trigger an update on each resource — for example, by adding a tag via az resource update, which triggers a re-evaluation and causes Append to inject its values. Modify with a remediation task is a better fit for retroactively applying changes to existing resources.

# Append policy assigned AFTER storage accounts already existed.
# The existing storage accounts do NOT have the appended IP rule.
# To trigger re-application, force an update on each resource:

# Option 1: Use az resource update with an arbitrary tag change to trigger ARM evaluation
# (The tag change causes a PUT that triggers the Append policy)
az resource update \
  --ids /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/sa-myapp \
  --set tags.policy-applied='true'

# Option 2: For storage accounts specifically, update network ACLs directly
az storage account update \
  --name sa-myapp \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --default-action Deny  # This triggers a PUT which causes Append to fire

# Option 3: For a large number of existing resources, use a script loop
az resource list \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query '[].id' -o tsv | while read RES_ID; do
  az resource update --ids "$RES_ID" --set tags.policy-trigger='$(date +%s)'
done

# Better: Use Modify with a remediation task for retroactive enforcement
# Append has no remediation task mechanism — remediation is a Modify/DINE feature only

Append has no remediation task support. If you need to retroactively apply changes to existing non-compliant resources, either switch to Modify (which supports remediation tasks) or manually trigger updates on each resource to cause Append to fire on the update operation.
Tracing Append Operations in Activity Logs

When Append modifies a request, the resulting resource is created with the appended values — but the activity log entry shows the resource creation or update as normal, not specifically as a policy-driven change. To verify that Append is working, compare the resource properties against what was in the original deployment template. The policy compliance state for resources affected by Append will be Compliant (the resource was created with the required values injected).

# Verify Append worked by checking the resource's network ACL rules
SA_NAME='sa-myapp'
RG='rg-prod'

# Check what IP rules are present (should include the policy-appended rule)
az storage account show \
  --name $SA_NAME \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --query 'networkRuleSet.{DefaultAction:defaultAction,IpRules:ipRules,VnetRules:virtualNetworkRules}' \
  -o json

# Check compliance state of the storage account for the append policy
az policy state list \
  --resource /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/$RG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/$SA_NAME \
  --filter "policyDefinitionAction eq 'append'" \
  --query '[].{Assignment:policyAssignmentName,State:complianceState}' \
  -o table

# Check the activity log for the storage account creation event
az monitor activity-log list \
  --resource-id /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/$RG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/$SA_NAME \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '1 hour ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ 2>/dev/null || date -u -v-1H +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --query '[].{Time:eventTimestamp,Op:operationName.value,Status:status.value}' \
  -o table

# View what policies are currently appending to a new resource creation
# Use deployment what-if to see the effective request after append:
az deployment group what-if \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --template-file sa-template.json \
  --parameters storageAccountName=$SA_NAME \
  --result-format FullResourcePayloads
# The what-if output shows the resource as it will be created after all append policies run
Common Append Mistakes
Mistake
Symptom
Fix
Using Append to correct a wrong value (not just fill in absent)
403 conflict error when user sets a different value for the same field
Use Modify with addOrReplace operation instead; Append cannot overwrite
Appending to a scalar field that is already set
403 conflict when deployer explicitly sets the field to any value
Add exists: false condition to only fire when field is absent
Expecting Append to apply to existing resources retroactively
Storage accounts created before policy was assigned missing the appended values
Trigger a resource update to re-fire Append, or switch to Modify with remediation task
Treating Append compliance state as enforcement
Non-compliant resources exist despite 'Append' policy
Append marks non-compliant on update conflicts; use Deny to prevent non-compliant resources
Using Append for tag enforcement when Modify would work better
Policy doesn't correct existing wrong tag values — only fills absent ones
Use Modify with addOrReplace — same add-if-absent behavior plus correction capability
Summary

The Append effect injects property values or array entries into an ARM request at evaluation time. For array fields, it merges the policy-specified values into the request's existing array — deployers retain their values and the policy's required values are added alongside. For scalar fields, it sets the value only when the field is absent; a conflict between the request's value and the policy's value produces a 403 error. Append does not require a managed identity (unlike Modify) because it mutates the request in-flight rather than making a separate API call after resource creation. For most tag and scalar property scenarios, prefer Modify over Append because Modify can correct wrong values not just fill in absent ones, and does not conflict when a different value is already specified.
